meta.purpose: AI corpus for mapping early PCI conversations to Desirable Properties
meta.usage: Pair with dp-context-for-pci-mapping.json. Ask the model to link each email to relevant DP IDs with relationship, summary, and confidence. Human review required before publishing.

emails.title: Meta-Layer Initiative Meeting with Vint Cerf
emails.author: Anon
emails.subject: Meta-Layer Initiative Meeting on Sept 16 with special guest Vint Cerf
emails.body:
From: Anon
Date: Mon, 09 Sep 2024 15:56:20 -0700
Subject: Meta-Layer Initiative Meeting on Sept 16 with special guest Vint Cerf
Dear PCI family,
I want to invite you to a ZOOM meeting on September 16th at 10a EDT with special guest Vint Cerf about developing an overlay environment that supports:
overlay applications active across all webpages
smart tags capable of attaching code to content
communities that are with you through the web experience.
The environment must be safe, secure, support fair value exchange and data sovereignty, and respect privacy, while safely enabling and utilizing AI. This environment should be protocol-agnostic.
Vint has graciously agreed to kickoff our discussion with some words of wisdom on building a meta-environment above the web, beginning with an application substrate.
if you would like to attend, feel free to reply directly to me.
Warmly
Anon

emails.title: Anticipating PCEs and Meta-Layer Alignment
emails.author: Cindy Mason
emails.subject: Re: [i4j Forum] Re: [PCI-C] natural monopolies and internet Google.
emails.body:
---- On Mon, 09 Sep 2024 17:07:45 -0700 Cindy Mason wrote ---

I'm unable to attend because it's at 7am PST. 

However, I'd like to give some input 

People are developing personal computing environments (PCEs) with private clouds to create more autonomy, privacy and control over their data, including  biodata, and to  avoid being part of decision making solely focused on the automatic essentia stereotype of  people that occurs in Gen AI and LLMs. It is expected these PCEs  will shield users from various issues on the net, like a personal guard  rail. 

I would hope that whatever new layer is developed, there will be an  anticipation  of these PCE systems. Especially given the on-going development of  data provenance  methods and the version inconsistency among the 10,000's of  downloads of various versions of ImageNet being used to create GenAIs,  which are  popping up everywhere. 

This is covered in a chapter of my forthcoming book, but I don't have it  edited yet,  I can give someone a rough draft if that helps. 

For some general info on this issue see  https://openreview.net/pdf?id=jh0ck1bPGF  The Nine Lives of ImageNet: A Sociotechnical Retrospective of a  Foundation Dataset and the  Limits of Automated Essentialism 

Best, 
Cindy

emails.title: Meta-Layer Initiative for Decentralized and Transparent Web
emails.author: Anon
emails.subject: Re: [i4j Forum] Re: [PCI-C] natural monopolies and internet Google.
emails.body:
---- Wed, 11 Sep 2024 16:23:50 -0700 -0700 Anon wrote ---

I agree completely. Human networks that exist in silos—whether decentralized or not—are destined to fail.

For human networks to thrive and have a planetary impact, they will need to:

Be composed of real people (with the option of pseudo-anonymity) in good standing with their communities.
Operate with decentralized structures, supported by AI-assisted community-based and personal moderation.
Remain active and interconnected across the entire web.
Have tools that allow them to meet, interact, and collaborate seamlessly across all digital spaces.
Have the ability to connect and annotate content across the web, enhancing collective knowledge.
Share access to a robust, interconnected information ecology.
Maintain control over their own data.

Without these principles, staying in silos with fake accounts, fake traffic, and fabricated virality will allow Big Tech to continue extracting value from our collective digital presence—feeding on our data and, in a sense, our very essence.

This is why we’re kicking off the Meta-Layer Initiative on September 16th. It’s time to create the tools and infrastructure to enable a truly decentralized, transparent, and impactful web.

Looking forward to continuing this conversation and making real progress.

emails.title: Metalevel Initiative: Challenges and Opportunities
emails.author: Paul Werbos
emails.subject: Re: [i4j Forum] Re: [PCI-C] natural monopolies and internet Google.
emails.body:
---- On Wed, 11 Sep 2024 16:35:06 -0700 Paul Werbos wrote ---

I do not yet know what "meta-level initiative" is intended to mean, or what it allows.

On the positive side, those words are 100% consistent with what I have been arguing for for many years.
In my post on Sustainable Intelligent Internet (SII), at werbos.com, I STARTED with the urgent need for a kind of metalevel platform...
BUT TO GO FROM GREAT WORDS to the ACTUAL REQUIRED DESIGN WORK... requires a level of determination, sincerity and knowledge which I
do not take for granted. Let us hope, let us help as much as we can, but let us also remember the sad lessons of how badly 
the nations of earth have risen to the challenge of preventing climate extinction. In 2019, Korean National TV broadcast my hour talk
on the big internet/IOT/AGI challenges requiring this kind of new platform...  It got lots of hits on youtube, Yan LeCun replied very visibly (as positively as he knew how,
but it is technically challenging)... BUT JUST THE PAST TWO MONTHS, two of the Google branches very suddenly tried to make it invisible.
They suppressed exactly the kind of technological reality needed to attain the objective -- just as old oil and gas people have successfully squashed what is now known about low cost reliable solar farms. (And neither Presidential candidate seems able to see past the cages they have been stuck in that way.)

And so... yes, metalevel initiative is exciting, real and deserving of maximum attention and support... but also sober memories of what happened after we were excited by EU noises about green new deals. We will see.

emails.title: AI-Enhanced Collective Intelligence for Global Challenges
emails.author: David Bray, PhD
emails.subject: Re: [i4j Forum] Re: [PCI-C] natural monopolies and internet Google.
emails.body:
---- On Thu, 12 Sep 2024 04:24:41 -0700 David Bray, PhD wrote ---

Anon and Vint - in a past life, I did a post-doc with Tom Malone at the MIT Center for Collective Intelligence on the topic of collective intelligence for very large/global networks. 

I am now here in Portugal for a series of meetings with NATO (and to give a talk + panel) alongside Tom Keller and what he’s doing with CrowdSmart. 

Interestingly, AI agents can overcome the scaling issues of collective intelligence and avoid some of the problems that cognitively human prefer smaller numbers of group to be their daily cohort. Yet in a globalized world, we can’t retreat or isolate ourselves from the world - even if some of the challenges of misinfo vs. disinfo have arisen because of that cognitive clash. Tom Keller believes, and incidentally some of the work I did in 2008 also suggests, collective intelligence paired with AI agents that attentively **listen to** human participants (instead of sell ads/spew info at us) can help overcome this in a more people-centered way. 

Some of my post-doc work by way of MIT in 2008, which was shared with Jason Matheny in 2009 to inform what eventually became IARPA’s Aggregative Contingent Estimation (ACE) activity - 
http://www.dbray.org/emory/images/Bray_Emergent_Collaboration.pdf

emails.title: The Overweb: Building a Decentralized Meta-Layer
emails.author: Anon
emails.subject: Re: [i4j Forum] Re: [PCI-C] natural monopolies and internet Google.
emails.body:
---- On Thu, 12 Sep 2024 12:23:45 -0700 Anon wrote ----

Thank you for your thoughts. We did deliberate quite a bit on what to call the meeting, as we're addressing abstract concepts that don’t yet exist in practice. I’ll share my perspective, and perhaps the group can refine the terminology.

We refer to the Metaweb as the decentralized space above the webpage. Since 2019, I’ve been working on what I call the Overweb—a potential meta-environment within this space. The Overweb aims to be a safe, decentralized environment where communities can meet, interact, and collaborate above webpages. In this space, individuals would own their data, have only one primary account, and receive fair value for their contributions.

With the Overweb, developers can create applications that operate above webpages. Participants can attach smart tags to content on any page, enriching the web experience.

What excites me most is that this layer feels like the current web but with added functionality. Today’s web already has multiple layers; however, they are controlled by the website owner. This centralization wasn’t the original intent—digital annotation predates the web, and Mosaic (the first widely distributed browser) supported web annotation.

What’s even better is that we don’t need a major technological breakthrough. Current internet protocols, browser technologies, cryptography, and AI are sufficient. The real breakthrough required is in people’s understanding, which is why this dialogue is so important.

At DWeb Camp, I introduced the idea as a meta-layer over the web to keep things simple and neutral. Feedback has been positive on the terms Metaweb and Overweb, and people also responded well to meta-layer, as reflected in the interest in this meeting. After further brainstorming (with help from CyberSavant, our GPT-based AI), we decided to call the kickoff meeting: "The Meta-Layer Initiative: Laying the Foundation." I’d love to hear any alternative ideas for this or future meetings.

I fully agree with your point that design work shouldn’t be underestimated, and thankfully, we have some brilliant minds involved. I hope this meeting will align us around a shared purpose and vision and lead to a collaborative design plan. For now, our focus is on the application substrate, and I think it’s key that we don’t rely on governments for this. Some may even feel threatened by the idea of a decentralized meta-layer—unlike Facebook, which seemed like a private entity building something DARPA had once explored, and has literally been a source for the U.S. government to gather information and censor people and narratives.

That said, I’m not ruling out government involvement. In fact, applying a meta-layer to government sites could be transformative.

Your story on Korean TV leaves me wondering about potential synergies between what you discussed and our vision for the Overweb. I see a meta-layer as instrumental, if not essential, to your MegaChallenge for the 21st Century: What is Mind? — particularly for building and understanding intelligence, both symbolic and subsymbolic.

Looking forward to hearing more of your thoughts!

emails.title: Augmented Conversations and AI-Assisted Collaboration in the Overweb
emails.author: Anon
emails.subject: Re: [i4j Forum] Re: [PCI-C] natural monopolies and internet Google.
emails.body:
---- On Thu, 12 Sep 2024 13:39:51 -0700 Anon wrote ----

Hi David,

Thank you for sharing your thoughts and insights—your experience at MIT’s Center for Collective Intelligence and the work with Tom Malone sound fascinating. The intersection of collective intelligence and AI agents is a timely and critical area, especially as we explore how to scale human collaboration effectively.

I agree that AI agents have immense potential in addressing the scaling challenges inherent in global networks. It’s particularly intriguing how they can help mitigate some of the cognitive limitations humans face when managing large-scale collectives. Your point about AI agents attentively listening to participants, rather than overwhelming us with information or ads, closely aligns with our vision for the Overweb. In the Overweb, we’re aiming to create an AI-assisted environment where humans engage meaningfully in a space that prioritizes privacy, data sovereignty, and fair value exchange, ensuring that AI enhances human capabilities rather than exploits attention.

This also brings to mind Engelbart protégé David Smith, whom I first encountered on a PCI call and covered in the Metaweb book. He described what he called the Augmented Conversation: a discussion between participants “extraordinarily enhanced” by computer tools and capabilities. In this model, the AI becomes a full participant in the conversation, allowing us to discuss and explore complex systems, datasets, and simulations as easily as we talk about the weather. A guarantee of shared truth is necessary: what I see, you must see. Any action taken that affects the shared simulation must reflect for all participants.

Without this, the communication channel is not trustworthy. The system must enable modification and extension of itself for all participants, allowing us to use it to extend its capabilities. Smith suggests a new kind of operating system built from scratch, focused on shared simulation and deep security.

I believe collective intelligence could greatly benefit from a shared information ecology, where related online information is connected at various levels of granularity, with explicit relationships (e.g., contradicting, supporting, citing). AI could assist by developing suggestions for these connections keeping a human-in-the-loop, while also relying on this global connected information network to improve the quality of its responses.

Your work with Jason Matheny on IARPA’s ACE activity resonates with my understanding of crowd wisdom. It seems like your research in emergent collaboration has strong synergies with what we’re proposing for the Meta-Layer Initiative. Specifically, we’re exploring how AI can support decentralized, real-time collaboration across interconnected, globally distributed networks, while ensuring that human agency remains central to decision-making.

I’d love to continue this conversation, especially around how these ideas could integrate with what we’re building in the Metaweb.

Warmly,
Anon 

P.S. Our GPT-based AI, CyberSavant, had some thoughts on the projects you mentioned and suggested that the Overweb could significantly enhance and amplify their impact by enabling seamless, interconnected collaboration. You can see the prompt and response here: https://chatgpt.com/share/317e92eb-2965-4d5c-a302-e87c6ba611ff

emails.title: Defining Goals for Effective Collective Intelligence
emails.author: David Bray, PhD
emails.subject: Re: [i4j Forum] Re: [PCI-C] natural monopolies and internet Google.
emails.body:
On Thu, 12 Sep 2024 14:04:03 -0700 David Bray, PhD wrote ---

Happy to explore collaborations Anon. Part of the beauty of Collective Intelligence is it actually gets better to more divergent the different perspectives of reality are amongst the participants - as long as the participants share the same overarching goal. If everyone thinks the same about reality, CI has marginal benefit vs. individual intelligence. And similarly if folks don’t share the same overarching goal, the different perspectives result in fragmentation. Which is why it is so essential to involve folks in defining upfront what the outcomes/goals we seek to pursue together - be it for an organization, a network of teams, or a society?

emails.title: Innovation Studios: Designing for Collective Intelligence in the Overweb
emails.author: Anon
emails.subject: Re: [i4j Forum] Re: [PCI-C] natural monopolies and internet Google.
emails.body:
---- On Thu, 12 Sep 2024 16:18:12 -0700 Anon wrote ----

Love it! For me, the holy grail would be everyone having access to a shared reality that connects all related and valuable content snippets. How does that happen? People get compensated for the connections they create and/or verify, based on the value those connections bring to the ecosystem. With this shared information ecosystem, as you said, collective intelligence emerges from the divergent perspectives, in this case of real people and potentially AIs that take on specific roles.

I’d love your input on building collective intelligence, or the potential to support it, into the application substrate where appropriate. I’ve been thinking about running "innovation studios" around specific subjects like collective intelligence, inspired by the Cynefin work of Dave Snowden, whom I was first introduced to through PCI.

The innovation studio process is designed to apply collective intelligence to the design of Overweb systems like identity, security, privacy, data sovereignty, and rewards. We will periodically publish calls for innovation collaborations around specific topics.

Participants and groups apply to an innovation studio focused on a specific topic, which has an overarching goal. Each group has 3-5 people, with at least one person aged 25 or younger and one person aged 50 or older, ensuring neuro-diversity and involving the younger generation in shaping the future of the Web. These innovation groups will work independently to develop novel proposals for governing, building, or operating aspects of the Overweb over the course of weeks to months.

Multiple groups can work on the same topic simultaneously, with proposals going through a synthesis process that is moderated by the facilitators of the innovation studio, designated experts, and/or the Overweb community at large. Participants would be eligible for a badge and/or rewards.

What do you think of this as a process for exploring collective intelligence in the context of a layer above the webpage?  I'm open to deviations and adjacent ideas.

emails.title: Future of Interaction: Moving Beyond Webpages
emails.author: David Bray, PhD
emails.subject: Re: [i4j Forum] Re: [PCI-C] natural monopolies and internet Google.
emails.body:
---- On Thu, 12 Sep 2024 16:33:05 -0700 David Bray, PhD wrote ----

It might be that webpages are going to go the way of landline phones - some people still have them, however they're increasingly rare.

Part of this is because what we're seeing now is a more interactive and conversational web, one where you can ask questions and talk to an AI agent using your voice, using text, and even using body language. Sure, we may still have things people can read electronically - however the newer patterns are youth using YouTube to look up  videos on "how to do XYZ" and thus watch while they hear while they learn.

So layering needs to account for a future where webpages aren't necessarily the dominant way of interacting. What would it mean to annotate over video, audio, and the like. Perhaps instead what we need is ways for my own AI agents to talk and interact with your AI agent - without requiring a centralized platform to host them both. After all, remember the early days of the WWW where anyone could launch their own web server/web pages fairly simply without having to spend a lot of money through a select handful of gatekeepers. Contrast that now with what is happening with GenAI which seems to have none of the early WWW flavor and more of the late-stage handful of a few gatekeepers for anyone wanting to jump in... perhaps we can change that?

emails.title: Acknowledging Contributions in Collaborative Spaces
emails.author: Cindy Mason
emails.subject: Re: [i4j Forum] Re: [PCI-C] natural monopolies and internet Google.
emails.body:
---- On Thu, 12 Sep 2024 17:26:44 -0700 Cindy Mason wrote ----

I might add, that its important to acknowledge others. 

There is new technology to monitor meetings to see who is actually contributing an idea... fascinating! 

I've stopped in some cased, contributing. Because we all need to feed our families and pay for where we live... acknowledging other people's input is important in keeping our careers alive.

emails.title: Collective Intelligence and Brain Synchronization
emails.author: Paul Werbos
emails.subject: Re: [i4j Forum] Re: [PCI-C] natural monopolies and internet Google.
emails.body:
---- On Thu, 12 Sep 2024 18:48:32 -0700 Paul Werbos wrote ----

COLLECTIVE INTEL:LIGENCE -- 

LIKE metaplatform... this is a truly fundamental and meaningful concept. I am very glad to see some emphasis on it. I hope we will have more serious dialogue on it in future.

HOWEVER: as with metaplatform, I see an analogy to the great cause of avoiding human climate extinction. It is important that we remember the gola, but equally important not to imagine it is easy.
In all three cases (metaplatform, collective intelligence and climate extinction) humanity needs really serious, scientifically grounded dialogue connecting many communities and ideas. So if we are starting such a dialogue... here are a few thoughts.

First, I hope we can all begin by fully accepting the collective intelligence has somethings to do with intelligence, which has something to do with the brain, and even with the work of Carl Jung. (I often thank Jelel Ezzine of UNESCO for his recommendation of Jung's Red Book, which is not light reading but is very deeply real and consistent with the best hard core neuroscience.)

THOSE who are serious about wanting to understand what collective intelligence can be, at its best, should consider submitting something to Frontiers in Neuroscience, which recently asked me to take over editorship of a new special issue, like the one which published my paper with Yeshua in the special issue dedicated to the heritage of Walter Freeman, one of the world's top two living systems neuroscientists before hiw death.
The story and key links are posted now at https://drpauljohn.blogspot.com/2024/09/position-paper-for-next-great-advances.html

I am cc'ing Robert Kozma, who coauthored Freeman's final book, summarizing what he had learned over decades about how brains really work. ***IF** the US government worked as well as NSF did when I worked there (1988-2015), they would have hired Robert to take over the research program in Cognitive Neuroscience, which is the best place to advance some of the very most crucial requirements in collective intelligence.

In those discussions, we had great contacts with Scott Kelso, Bressler and Tuller (all still alive and active, so far as I know). (In fact, Yeshua will be visiting the US again soon; in the past the base in Florida was part of the story.

COLLECTIVE INTELLIGENCE (by ordinary brain mechanisms) is closely connected to BRAIN SYNCHRONIZATION, a field of research which those three pioneered but which demands a lot more extension, analysis and application.
Using EEG measures, they were able to measure N-person EEG synchronization effects for people very effectively cooperating in challenging cognitive tasks.

Some of you may remember folks who say: ":If you can't measure it, you can't support and amplify it." That is part of the research challenge. But of course, a range of tasks and measures is needed.
It can even be applied to human-animal cooperation; I have seen huskies in Alaska working with the National Park Service, and special horses in Iceland, which really do deserve more understanding and research.   
  
But as I said in the blog post... I will never forget my Harvard PhD advisor, Karl Deutsch, who in my view was the most crucial thought leader behind the European Union itself. Really. Back then, in that group, I even got to use the letterhead where he and Kissinger also appeared (and Raiffa, I think...). His books on nationalism and social communication and community were more famous, but HE was most proud of this own PhD thesis which was published as The Nerves of Government. Modeling human collective intelligence using neural network concepts is an extremely useful viewpoint to include as PART of our new perspective.

Best of luck in this hugely important enterprise...

emails.title: Cross-Cultural and Multilingual AI Tools

emails.title: Digital Cognitive Pathways and Metaweb Evolution
emails.author: Anon
emails.subject: Re: [i4j Forum] Re: [PCI-C] natural monopolies and internet Google.
emails.body:
---- On Fri, 13 Sep 2024 08:23:09 -0700 Anon wrote ----

Hi David,

Thank you again for your insightful response. It spurred us to think deeply about how we describe and build the layers of the Metaweb. Your point about moving beyond traditional webpages and considering other forms of interaction—like video, audio, and AI-driven interfaces—resonated with us.

In fact, your comments have led us to develop a new abstraction framework we're calling Digital Cognitive Pathways. This framework helps us better conceptualize how users, or Agents (or Explorers), navigate different Terrains (or Mediums) and interact with Artifacts (or Elements) of content across the entire digital space, whether it's web-based, VR, AR, or even AI-driven interfaces.

Here’s the current working model:

Agent (or Explorer): The entity (human, AI, etc.) that navigates through the digital space.
Terrain (or Medium): The environment being navigated, such as a webpage, a VR world, or an audio stream.
Artifact (or Element): The specific content or media being interacted with or annotated, whether it's a text snippet, a video clip, or an interactive object in a virtual space.

One exciting challenge you raised was how to enable annotations across all forms of media—regardless of where or how they’re accessed (via browser, AI, or VR/AR). This aligns well with the Metaweb’s evolution, where both the terrain(the environment) and the artifact (the content) would be hashed. This would allow us to uniquely identify them and track if they’ve changed, ensuring that annotations are consistent across all mediums. For example, the same video segment, whether it's embedded in a webpage, part of a virtual world, or directly accessed by an AI, would bring up the same annotations. This type of hashing would create a universal annotation layer across the web, VR, AR, and AI-driven interactions.

You also brought up a crucial point about AI agents communicating independently without relying on centralized platforms. This aligns perfectly with the Metaweb's decentralized goals, where interactions are not controlled by gatekeepers. In the Metaweb, AI agents can indeed interact directly, but all their interactions would need to be logged on-chain in a human-readable format, as described in the Metaweb book. This ensures transparency, trust, and accountability while preserving the decentralized nature of the ecosystem. We’re keen to explore how AI agents can freely engage across decentralized terrains within these digital cognitive pathways while maintaining this necessary transparency.

We’d love to hear your thoughts on this framework, as we’re still refining it. How do you think it could be expanded or adjusted to fit the future you envision, where conversational and interactive web experiences dominate? And how do you see these digital pathways enhancing the collective intelligence work you're engaged in?

Looking forward to continuing this conversation and seeing how these ideas can evolve together.

emails.title: Recognizing and Rewarding Contributions in the Metaweb
emails.author: Anon
emails.subject: Re: [i4j Forum] Re: [PCI-C] natural monopolies and internet Google.
emails.body:
---- On Fri, 13 Sep 2024 08:39:53 -0700 Anon wrote ----

Hi Cindi 
Thank you for your thoughtful response—it resonates with me, especially your point about acknowledgment. It’s true, recognition is critical not only for fostering collaboration but also for keeping people engaged and motivated in their work. Your comments about the importance of recognizing contributions and the technology that can track input inspire us to take this even further. 

As we develop the Metaweb and its innovation processes, we can explore ways to incorporate fiat or token-based rewards tied directly to contributions. This would create a transparent, decentralized system that ensures everyone’s input is not only acknowledged but also fairly compensated. The idea would be to implement a contribution tracking mechanism (potentially on-chain) that monitors participation and ensures proper attribution for valuable insights, much like the new technologies you mentioned for monitoring meetings. 

Overall, we want to make sure that Metaweb contributors are recognized and rewarded for the ideas they bring to the table, whether through creating connections, verifying content, or, in the case of the innovation studio, contributing to the design and implementation of the application substrate. This would reinforce the goal of maintaining an open, fair, and incentivized environment where people are motivated to contribute, knowing that their input will be both seen and compensated.

I’d love to hear your thoughts on this, and if you have any suggestions for how best to implement such a system!

emails.title: Call for Ideas: Help Shape the Future of the Web
emails.author: Anon
emails.subject: [PCI-C] Call for Ideas: Help Shape the Future of the Web
emails.body:
From: Anon
To: "PCI-Community-Calls"
Date: Fri, 04 Oct 2024 14:10:10 -0700
Subject: Call for Ideas: Help Shape the Future of the Web

Dear PCI Community
I’m sorry I couldn’t attend the Open Mic on Tuesday. Had I been there, I would have shared details about the first Meta-Layer meeting two weeks ago and the exciting Call for Ideas we’re launching today about the future of the Web. Please find the announcement of the Meta-Layer's Call for Ideas on the future of the Web on Substack at Help Shape the Future of the Web.

I formally invite all the PCI community and staff to contribute their ideas in this initial formative stage of the Meta-Layer Initiative. I believe the PCI community is uniquely positioned to contribute to this, as the meta-environment is being designed to be a truly people-centered layer. 

Unfortunately, on Tuesday am, I was deep in coding my first satplication on Fractal Bitcoin. I had been at it since 5a. A satplication is the name I am giving decentralized applications that run code situated on satoshis, the atomic unit of Bitcoin. The project I’m working on is a dynamic ordinal NFT collection called the Fractal Goblin Club, where I serve as both generative artist and ordinal developer. We were launching the very next day.

That said, satplications represent a fascinating approach to building decentralized systems, and I’ll share more about this concept as I continue to develop it. My next satplication will be a digital badge for those who contribute ideas to the call for input.

Thank you for your time and attention!

Anon

PS, Thanks PCI for showing up so strong at the meeting!

emails.title: AI and Humanity's Deepest Problems: Love and Compassion
emails.author: Cindy Mason
emails.body:
---- On Sun, 13 Oct 2024 08:27:02 -0700 Cindy Mason wrote ---

and I work for the day when computers (AI, VR, gaming, etc.) can help mankind with its deepest problems - how to love, how to forgive, and be compassionate....

could it be that if someone has just been loved enough they may themselves be able to love?

if we go to mars, and beyond, we must find ways of supporting this incredible part of our humanity. love moves mountains. but hate destroys. can AI help us overcome this part of ourselves?

Perhaps we need to give AI a chance to digest philosophy....

Hoping to have the book (Artificial Compassion) finished by end of the year...

Wish me luck.

emails.title: Fostering Serendipitous Digital Connections through the Meta-Layer
emails.author: Anon
emails.subject: Re: [PCI-C] The Morning: What the A.I. Nobels teach us about humanity
emails.body:
============ Forwarded message ============
From: Anon
Date: Tue, 15 Oct 2024 10:23:06 -0700
============ Forwarded message ============

First, I'd like to acknowledge and thank Cindy Mason for her inspiring work on humanizing computing, particularly in her exploration of artificial compassion. As we contemplate the future of digital technologies and their potential to address some of humanity’s deepest emotional and relational needs, your dedication to this space is deeply motivating. I believe we share a common goal: leveraging technology not just for efficiency, but to foster love, empathy, and meaningful human connection.

I’m convinced--through Digital Destiny Theory--that there are hundreds, if not thousands, of people in the digital world we are destined to meet, reconnect with, or collaborate with, whose contributions could help shape the course of our lives for the better. These connections are not random; they are rooted in shared passions, purposes, and experiences. However, they often remain unrealized in the physical world. How many times have we encountered an old friend or discovered a mutual connection and exclaimed, “It’s a small world!”? In reality, though, the digital world is vast and filled with people we’re meant to cross paths with. We often exist in parallel, visiting the same virtual spaces--reading the same articles, donating to the same causes, or engaging with the same ideas--without ever directly interacting.

The opportunity to make these connections lies in digital spaces, but not in the way we might currently imagine. The metaverse, while promising, is in its infancy and lacks sufficient safety measures. Instead, I believe the real potential lies in what I call the meta-layer--a conceptual space above traditional webpages. This layer provides the framework for serendipitous encounters that are both safe and meaningful. Imagine visiting a website, and instead of merely consuming content, you can see and engage with others who are also there, perhaps people you already know, or people who share purposes and are seeking collaboration.

In this meta-layer, we move beyond the dangers of social media and dating apps--spaces often filled with scammers and predators--and into a realm of authentic connection, where people are accountable and interactions are purpose-driven. Much like encountering a kindred spirit in a cafe, where you strike up a conversation about a shared interest, the meta-layer allows us to meet, interact, and collaborate in digital spaces. After all, when we enter a website, why shouldn’t we also encounter those who share our thoughts, passions, and ideas?

This theory suggests that there are connections we are meant to make in the digital realm—because how else would we meet? Just as the physical world offers the occasional serendipitous encounter, the digital world, if structured thoughtfully, should provide the vastly more opportunities for connection, interaction, and collaboration.

We’ve already begun to see early forms of this in collaborative spaces and online communities, but these often feel siloed and limited by the constraints of current technology. This is where the idea of the Metaweb comes into play--a hyper-dimensional layer that exists above the current web and fosters human connection, trust, and collaborative intelligence. The Metaweb, as outlined by Bridgit DAO, creates a decentralized public space that can help us meet others with similar passions, reduce scams and disinformation, and enable truly meaningful interactions​.

As Gary Kreps so insightfully pointed out, the evolution of computing has often focused on tasks like organizing, calculating, and summarizing information—bureaucratic, content-oriented goals that, while valuable, do little to address the deeper human needs for connection, compassion, and empathy​. The potential for computing to foster humanistic goals, such as caring, friendship, and shared purpose, has only begun to be explored. This meta-layer concept ties directly into that idea—using the digital space not just for efficiency, but for meaningful interpersonal connections that enrich our lives.

This space offers a more relational form of computing, one that addresses core human needs for belonging, affection, and collaboration, as highlighted in Will Schutz’s theory of interpersonal needs​. Imagine logging onto a webpage and seeing that a friend is on the same page, reading the same article, or engaging with the same content. This could spark conversations or collaborations that might otherwise never have occurred, creating a sense of shared experience even in digital isolation.

Moreover, as we continue to face growing challenges in digital safety--such as the spread of misinformation, online harassment, and privacy violations--the Metaweb’s decentralized structure could help mitigate these issues. By providing verifiable identities, contextual information, and accountability, the meta-layer fosters a safer and more trustworthy environment for these digital encounters to flourish.

Cindy, I believe that what you’re working toward—using technology to help humanity tackle its deepest problems, such as how to love, forgive, and be compassionate—is exactly the kind of human-centered future we should be striving for. We’re moving toward a world where computing isn’t just a tool for efficiency, but a platform for growth, connection, and mutual support. AI, gaming, VR, and digital platforms should be designed to facilitate our better selves, as your work so profoundly suggests. The more we give space to these humanistic applications of technology, the more we can build a compassionate, connected future that benefits us all.

The digital world can--and should--be a place where we meet others who will walk alongside us in our journey, helping us grow and find purpose. Whether it's through the Metaweb, the meta-layer, or new, innovative spaces yet to be developed, I’m certain that these connections are out there, waiting to happen.

emails.title: Navigating Existential Threats with AGI and Meta-Frameworks
emails.author: Paul Werbos
emails.subject: [PCI-C] Join Us for Meta-Layer Working Sessions @mdash; Let's Shape the Future of the Web Together
emails.body:
---- On Fri, 18 Oct 2024 07:26:38 -0700 Paul Werbos wrote ---

Thank you very much, Anon!

A few years ago, I would have been ecstatic to see your important focused efforts absolutely central to the first existential threat to humanity on my list attached, which I promise never to forget.

I still hope for the best, and will try to help.

But to live up to this challenge and survive, PART of the challenge is to keep updating our cognitive maps of where we are and what threads are available in the future. This really is a "minefield" decision challenge.
 
As intelligent systems of all kinds proliferate, from AI to AGI, we cannot expect a metaframework to work unless it too possesses the AGI level of intelligence (which simply means a general ability to learn hope its environment works and  how to learn a strategy to advance SOME purpose or values, which ultimately need to map into cardinal utility function U as Von Neumann and his followers have explained in depth).  "MINEFIELD" type decision problems are part of the mathematical specification of what such intelligent systems are asked to address; only with the material in my new patent, approved and published three days ago (https://patents.justia.com/inventor/paul-j-werbos), can we build AGI which directly address how difficult such problems are.. In truth, even human brains are evolved to handle a level of intelligence held together by classical physics, which is inherently limited in how far it can go in surviving millions of pitfalls ahead of us. 

More seriously, and more immediately, networks of HUMANS trying to manage the new minefield of new technologies (AGI and finance and weapons and communications and observation) now open to us are displaying their limitations every day now, as possibilities for new global wars (led in great part by human leaders deluded enough to imagine they know the way forward enough to allow humans to survive) grow very quickly.  EVEN THE CLASSICAL FORMS OF AGI (as in my earlier patents, listed in the Justia file) were based on years of mathematical work learning how to prevent general conflict of interest (COI) effects from leading to gross instabilities. In global financial systems, for example, new gross instabilities dur to COI could already cause  stunning bankruptcy problems ... like the breakup of GE, the current tremens of Boeing, and visible decays of Google and Meta and other major IT company leaderships, which are making changges in internet plans NOW whihc seriously threaten human life.  (For example, the growing overreliance on nuclear fission to power server farms is NOT the most economic way technically, and far more dangerous to global security, as I learned through https://new.nsf.gov/funding/opportunities/ari-joint-domestic-nuclear-detection-office-national-science/503223/nsf13-554) . Human networks of COI are the main explanation behind this very urgent and huge decision error.)

Even in CLASSICAL RLADP decision designs, we know how to totally eliminate that kind of COI, as my earlier patents reflected. (The best "Iron Dome" missile interception tools in use today are based on that kind of RLADP, implemented by Balakrishnan whom I funded to use that math in that application, but COI led Lockheed et al to NOT use the next generation, which would have saved hundreds or thousands of lives already in the Middle east.)  AND SO...  The "privacy and security" provisions in a new metaframework or Sustainable Intelligent Internet organizing platform  should account for the kinds of information flow restrictions which follow from using RLADP intelligence as the central organizing principle, limiting BOTH apps AND humans. This does not have to wait for quantum AGI (QAGI), but QAGI can take this further, especially by allowing cybersecurity capabilities which are essential to this and to many sensitive applications (again like electric power, financial and contract networks, and military kinds of things).

========

I am amazed that  adult human brains are so untrained that they are not immediately horrified by two working examples of what EXISTING AGI systems are already doing to human personal life and security.

The most astounding examples are the recent assassinations by Israel and, even more, the more powerful systems disempowering most humans in China today (perhaps even including Xi himself, who is not so all-powerful as Westerners often imagine).

Why are people not asking themselves: if this is UNIVERSAL AGI technology, available all over the world, some of it much more powerful than what Israel knows about, who on the earth is safe, or empowered , or free even to think for themselves, or, yes, to exercise freedom of spiritual growth, which itself can be perverted to serve deluded and narcissistic would-be tyrants? (There is a physicians' desk reference on human neuroses which is VERY relevant to the dynamics of human networks, like the delusions which cause tyranny.) This technology is HERE NOW!!!

I will probably fail to cc Karl Schroeder, who once ran the Canada node of the Millennium Project,  who SHOULD be part of this effort, who has a great novel Stealing Worlds which can help us humans overcome our mental blocks and envision better what this kind of capability really means -- AND ALSO why conventional legal and regulatory networks MAKE THINGS WORSE if not properly subordinated.. 

For example, if Musk agrees to "solve" COI problems in human networks by inserting government regulators into filtering human speech... as Trump tried to enforce late in his Presidency when the "section 450" issue came up... that exactly AMPLIFIES the growing life-threatening instabilities I hinted at above. COI and AGI-based networks for information and foresight are essential foundations. 

It gets even scarier. I have read stories about financial companies organizing elite groups to fire 90% of their employees, made unnecessary as AGI take over the valuation job (assessing U and variables which RLADP uses based on U, such as "shadow prices" and assessed market values)... only to learn in the end that THEIR jobs will be removed, and only owners still there. MORE EFFICIENT SII systems and apps would at least INCLUDE humans, but today's pressures to MOVE FAST prevent such developments. Human empowerment is suffering badly already, due to the ways in which development is now proceeding. It is what we call a "Nash equilibrium.' We need platforms which can either enforce Pareto optimality, OR at least move us to market designs which make the Nassdh equilibria more sustainable for humans.   

... But at this time, I myself have a fast growing problem of trust... limiting what I can say. Especially about things like physics or global conflicts. 

Best of luck...

emails.title: Human-Centered AI: A Critical Perspective
emails.author: Cindy Mason
emails.body:
---- Fri, 25 Oct 2024 02:08:36 -0700 Cindy Mason wrote ---

I imagine the group will be sorting out terms and conditions. As someone who has been in both AI/ML and mixed disciplinary worlds, I see words and phrases bandied about outside of AI/ML, but they have a VERY different meaning.

One phrase in particular makes me cringe when I hear it outside of ML circles: human-centered or people-centered.

Here is a section from my upcoming book, where I write about the way ML experts view this term. The short version is: do not assume this means caring about you or your data.

From Chapter 5, Designing Artificial Compassion Technology, ISBN: 9798882594984, Forthcoming

Human Centered AI - What does it really mean? It literally means nothing, unless you understand something about who is saying it and what they are doing with your data. To machine learning researchers and marketing experts “human centered” or “people centered” means something completely different than it does to the average person. For a machine learning expert, human centered does not imply caring. It is wrong to assume this. And it is an extremely important point. Outside of machine learning and advanced marketing, the phrase “human centered” AI or “people centered” AI is a term used in the same way as the advertising phrase “natural ingredients.”

Data about people’s movements, feelings, and shopping habits can be called human centered. Over the past decade or so, a great deal of data has been (and continues to be) quietly gathered on people without their knowledge. It has created what is called the data economy and it is worth more than gold or oil. This data is often integrated with more “human centered” data and can be processed by AI systems for many reasons.

For example, it can be used for marketing targeted ads to individuals or it can be sold to third parties. As we saw in the last chapter, large corporations have gone to incredible lengths to build data archives, machines and algorithms. We would like to imagine that their use of the phrase “human centered” or “people centered” means caring for you, but it literally only means that the data is about people and how they can use it for profit. For ACT systems, caring and protecting people includes caring and protecting their data, too. Later in the book, we will learn about Algorithmic and Data Duty of Care.

Remember that when you hear the phrase, “human centered” to look closely at what and who is using this data. In some regions of the world, people now live in total surveillance and are powerless on how that data is used by governments, institutions or corporations. Chapter 15 contains a number of ways to investigate and protect your privacy.

Another phrase worth noting:

From Chapter 5, Designing Artificial Compassion Technology, ISBN: 9798882594984, Forthcoming

Human Machine Collaboration - For many ML Programmers and data scientists, human machine collaboration refers to the use of people generated documents, images, sounds or webpages for algorithms and models. This perspective on "collaboration" is held only within the Machine Learning community. Outside of the Machine Learning community, this is called stealing or copyright violation. There is an unwritten assumption within Machine Learning culture that everyone who puts data online is offering it up to a Machine Learning algorithm. This has quickly caused serious social issues to arise. For example, when taking samples of people’s voices that are then used to make deep fakes (e.g. Scarlett Johansson). This blindspot or mismatch is being brought to court under a developing new section of legal cases entitled Generative AI Litigation (Valente et al. 2023; Walsh 2023; Vondle and Lee 2023).

Best wishes,
Cindy

ps. I think what you're doing is very important. I owe a big report to someone by year's end,
so I should avoid distracting myself... its very tempting. I hope this is helpful..
discussions welcome, as always!

emails.title: Legal Liabilities in the Meta-Layer
emails.author: Brian Donohue
emails.body:
---- On Mon, 28 Oct 2024 06:04:55 -0700 Brian Donohue wrote ----

The meta-layer includes all the legal rights and legal liabilities of the complicated legal system in the US, and any country where the meta-layer touches human beings. Patents, copyrights, trade secrets, trademarks, modified by the intricate license agreements, and long decided case law, all attach financial benefits and financial risks and burdens.

The meta-layer suggests that machines can do so much more. There can be a natural feeling of empowerment and trail-blazing galore.  

However, as Brian Donohue J.D., M.B.A. (Information Technology),  a  federal litigator of early technology, the above thinking is a false aberration. The law is still the law. Legal liability does not flow upstream to the machine - legal liability  flows upstream to the human author, or the person, or company which releases the technology.

Ole Irish adage:
" It's not what you don't know that will get you---
                         it's what you don't know, you don't know, that will!"

emails.title: Strategic Opportunity for Internet Reform with New Leadership
emails.author: Anon
emails.subject: Elevating the Need to Fix the Internet: A Strategic Opportunity with the Changing of the Guard
emails.body:
============ Forwarded message ============
From: Anon
Date: Mon, 28 Oct 2024 16:18:27 -0700
Subject: [PCI-C] ​Elevating the Need to Fix the Internet: A Strategic Opportunity with the Changing of the Guard
============ Forwarded message ============

Esteemed colleagues,

With the upcoming change in leadership, regardless of who wins the presidency, we have a unique opportunity to elevate the urgent need to fix the internet. As the internet plays an increasingly central role in shaping public discourse and AI is coming of age, it becomes critical to address systemic issues like **information centralization, censorship, and privacy erosion**---issues that threaten both democracy and personal freedoms.

One of the **most potent levers** for this effort is **free speech**, protected under the First Amendment. Ensuring freedom of expression in the digital age requires rethinking our information infrastructure to foster **decentralized, open networks **where individuals can speak, engage, and innovate without fear of censorship or monopolistic control. But **free speech is just one part of the equation**. Below are key constitutional and strategic levers to consider as part of this campaign:

1.  **Free Speech (First Amendment):**

    -   Decentralizing information safeguards against **censorship by platform monopolies** and ensures that public discourse remains vibrant and diverse.

    -   Prevents the **bottlenecking of information flow**, where only a few actors control the narrative.

2.  **Right to Privacy (Fourth Amendment):**

    -   Current data practices violate the spirit of privacy protections by allowing unchecked surveillance by platforms and governments.

    -   Decentralized technologies return **data sovereignty to individuals**, allowing them to control their digital footprint.

3.  **Due Process and Transparency (Fifth and Fourteenth Amendments):**

    -   Online decisions about content moderation, account suspensions, and data access lack transparency or consistent recourse.

    -   A decentralized internet model can build in **transparent, community-based governance**.

4.  **Freedom of Association (First Amendment):**

    -   Individuals must be free to form communities and networks across the web without being constrained by platform-specific silos.

    -   Decentralization allows **meta-communities** to emerge organically across multiple platforms, facilitating democratic organization and participation.

That being said here are some key points for the letter:

-   **Combating Misinformation and Building Trust: **A decentralized internet reduces the risks of manipulation by a few actors, promoting **information integrity **through transparency, redundancy, and open protocols.

-   **Data Sovereignty and Digital Rights: **Decentralizing data ensures that individuals and communities, not corporations, control their personal information, promoting **privacy, trust, and autonomy**.

-   **Economic Opportunities and Resilience: **Decentralization fosters **competitive innovation** by preventing monopolies and lowering the barriers to entry for new digital enterprises. This model aligns with efforts to create **resilient digital economies** that are community-driven and sustainable.

-   **Civic Participation and a New Digital Democracy: **An open, decentralized web encourages **active citizenship** and fosters environments where **new forms of civic engagement** can thrive. A decentralized system ensures that **everyone has equal access** to participate in the digital commons, free from gatekeeping.

-   **AI and Digital Ethics: **As AI continues to evolve, **decentralized systems allow communities to oversee and govern AI usage **transparently, reducing the risks of bias and manipulation.

**A Timely and Historic Moment**

As we move toward a new presidency, this open letter to the incoming administration could lay the foundation for **transformative change**. Beyond technical and policy interventions, it must emphasize the **importance of individual rights, public trust, and social resilience** in shaping a future-proof information ecosystem.

This is an opportunity to **lead a national and global movement toward digital decentralization**, ensuring that the internet evolves into a tool for empowerment, not control. With support from the right stakeholders---including government, civil society, and technical communities---we can push for reforms that will protect democratic values and ensure **open, trusted information spaces for generations to come**.

We look forward to your thoughts on framing these ideas into a powerful message to the next president, making **decentralization** not just a technical necessity, but a **moral imperative** for the future of democracy.

The idea would be to post the letter on Bitcoin, provide a means for people to endorse the letter, and spread it via social media.

What do you think: Is this a pipe dream or could something come of it? What points would be important to have in the open letter?

emails.title: Ensuring Balance and Preventing Weaponization of Principles
emails.author: David Bray, PhD
emails.subject: Elevating the Need to Fix the Internet: A Strategic Opportunity with the Changing of the Guard
emails.body:
---- On Mon, 28 Oct 2024 17:05:33 -0700 David Bray wrote ----

All for what you propose Anon - one question though, do you have folks trying to figure out how all these well-intended principles could be used against free societies and/or weaponized?

Because we may be realizing that there's "balance to all things" and rarely in this universe is any one thing an absolute?

emails.title: Preserving Real History and Strengthening Cybersecurity
emails.author: Paul Werbos
emails.subject: Elevating the Need to Fix the Internet: A Strategic Opportunity with the Changing of the Guard
emails.body:
---- Tue, 29 Oct 2024 11:01:05 -0700 Paul Werbos wrote ---

Thank you, Brian!

More and more, as I watch a world spinning into oblivion with a push to very dangerous fake history stories (like what you are working to debunk),...

I often wish we had a really good archive somewhere of what has REALLY been happening across so many fields!! I even contacted an LDS archivist to try to arrange storage of the many files in my own possession...

For 1977... I do not change the story, but I enjoy reminiscing a bit. Please forgive! In 1976, I moved from Laurel Maryland (where I lived just one year, in a rented townhouse next to that of an NSA sargeant) to college Park, Maryland, where I was a co-PI on a DARPA grant on conflict prediction and resolution. We installed a then-new Decwriter in my house, which I used in 1977 or so. (Details are in my files and old  magnetic tapes). Through DARPA, I connected to the brand new internet, with 100-200 favored people like me using it for real work. I was amazed to see names like Hal Puthoff and Targ of SRI on the list, but THEY never replied to  email. I was also amazed to see a novel, Starfire, by one of their experimental subjects, Ingo swann, depicting what we were doing in College Park much more closely (but of course not 100%) than I expected.  My MAIN use of the system was to connect to WWMCCS in the bowels of the Pentagon, both electronically and by magnetic tape.

WWMCCS used Honeywell Multics operating systems with provably unbreakable security, using theorems in the "orange book" developed by a professor at Amherst, whom I exchanged emails with until his relatively recent untimely death.  It is VERY understandable that the "rainbow book" technology which that led to has been very limited in the US, but enough has been published that other nations moved fast enough to be well ahead of the US in many such fields. That is one of the reasons why I keep supporting the call for open, transparent international platforms to integrate and balance the internet subnetworks, and to hold a new system for "seeing the sky" giving warning to all nations of the earth. Just yesterday I witnessed a very serious cybersecurity breach, atttacking a Zoom conversation led by Metta Spencer of Peace Magazine, such that SHE, the victim, may have all her work over decades  removed form visibility (at least until after months of investigation which they may not even be able to fulfill), as punishment. Better cybersecurity for the growing number of innocent victims using the internet is becoming ever more essential to human survival.

emails.title: Designing a Balanced Meta-Layer to Prevent Principle Weaponization
emails.author: Anon
emails.subject: Elevating the Need to Fix the Internet: A Strategic Opportunity with the Changing of the Guard
emails.body:
---- On Tue 2024-10-29 12:42 -0700 Daveed Benjamin wrote ----

Thanks for raising that important question, David. You’reabsolutely right—principles can be weaponized, and ensuring thatdoesn’t happen is crucial.

What sets a meta-layer apart is that we have the opportunity to designthe system intentionally—striking the right balance between privacy,safety, agency, and accountability. Unlike the open internet, wherewe’re often reacting to issues, a meta-layer allows us to establishthe rules proactively from the outset.

This approach helps us avoid the extremes—no one principle becomesabsolute—while remaining adaptive to new risks. A couple of weeksago, Paul Werbos shared some exciting ideas in this group on how wemight embed intelligence in a meta-layer for adaptive governance.Would love to hear your thoughts on how we can refine these frameworksfurther to stay ahead of potential misuse.

emails.title: Critique of Decentralization and Bitcoin in Meta-Layer Principles
emails.author: Dave Troy
emails.subject: Elevating the Need to Fix the Internet: A Strategic Opportunity with the Changing of the Guard
emails.body:
---- Tue, 29 Oct 2024 14:18:01 -0700 Dave Troy wrote ---

Bringing Bitcoin into such a declaration creates many problems, and will limit reach of your effort. I recommend the following reading list:

https://www.upress.umn.edu/9781517901806/the-politics-of-bitcoin

https://papers.ssrn.com/sol3/papers.cfm?abstract_id=4985877

https://washingtonspectator.org/paranoia-on-parade

https://washingtonspectator.org/peter-thiel-and-the-american-apocalypse

The principles you outline are obviously well intentioned, but principled people can disagree on the degree to which “decentralization” is an unalloyed good, which indeed is a key strength of the design of the internet. More people need to become familiar with the degree to which Bitcoin (and similar designs) incorporate and mandate zero-sum games that are harmful to society, and antagonistic to any sort of “people centered internet.”

Best,
Dave

emails.title: Incorporating Security and Privacy in the Meta-Layer
emails.author: Vandana Upadhyay
emails.subject: Elevating the Need to Fix the Internet: A Strategic Opportunity with the Changing of the Guard
emails.body:
---- On Tue, 29 Oct 2024 17:18:34 -0700 Vandana Upadhyay wrote ---- 

Great discussion and thank for the opportunity to offer input. Fair warning ;my comments below stem from a limited perspective of a  startup founder addressing IoT security and way removed from the advanced/visionary thinking that happens in this forum.

I actually drafted an email last year to circulate here, that in view of the 50th Anniversary of the internet, it was time to examine the design choices/tradeoff that were made in internet's design due to resource constraints that were prevalent at that time and to revisit them.

Security was one thing that was seconded to performance.  From the annals of history, quote

....Dr. Howard Shrobe (MIT professor) and others note that the Internet’s basic design decisions were made when computer hardware was significantly more expensive than it is today. Forty years later, the consequences of decisions made in those resource-constrained days remain. “Everything was built with performance, not security, in mind,” Dr. Shrobe said. “We left it to programmers to incorporate security into every line of code they wrote. .” I recall reading about ten years ago (that's why cannot cite the exact reference), that the discusison on security is documented in some internal ARPANET memos....one observation was that since one is sending data only to the next floor and within the building, one does not need security since the building itself was physically secure. No one then thought how intenet would expand and connect not only people and entities around the world but also every little object of day to day use.

So, perhaps we start with incorporating security and privacy in the new meta layer model. And it all starts with identities, an area that I worked in for past few years. Many would contest that verifiable identities may violate, a core principle of the internet, i.e anonymity. And one could argue that given how we are tracked all the time, is there still any anonymity left on the internet. The difference is that those with resources such as big corporations/data brokers/authoritarian regimes/malicious actors all know in minute detail who we are, where we go, what we do, who we talk to etc etc.... it's just that our neighbor or family member may not know that we are posting violent content on X,  

There could be an aspect of meta layer that allows people/devices/ to be anonymous and to be authenticated when needed. For example, no one can use a mobile network without the SIM being authenticated by the network. But no one other than the network operator knows that the SIM is associated with a particular phone number and a human behind it.  But without SIm authentication noone can be on the network. There may not be one idetnity but multiple identities depending on context and usage.

Many years ago, DARPA had started the Clean Slate initiative to address some of these new security challenges stemming from the scale of the internet, CUSTARD was another such program. I could not find any definitive assessment of what difference these prgrams made and to what extent the research was converted into commercial products.

From a commerical perspective I can say that, while trying to persuade companies to implement robust identity/authentication in their IoT device, we often hit the wall because security was seen as a cost not a competitive differentiator. So until the business value aspect is addressed, we will not see investment/implementation of security/privacy provisions unless regulatory mechanisms are used which may run counter to the spirit of the internet.

Thank you

emails.title: Customizable Software Agents for Security and Privacy
emails.author: Cindy Mason
emails.subject: Elevating the Need to Fix the Internet: A Strategic Opportunity with the Changing of the Guard
emails.body:
---- Tue, 29 Oct 2024 18:51:57 -0700 Cindy Mason wrote ---

Thank you Vandana for bringing up this important perspective and
suggestion!!

I've just been hammering out an architecture that encapsulates security functionality in customizable personal software agents...

IMHO, We already need a layer for agents. It could be part of the application services layer, or even application layer. But agents need to be registered and accountable. Perhaps even licensed?
These agents can interface between you and your internal systems (which may also have an agent) and between you and external systems/agents.
It is identified on the net similar to a software identification registration system.

In chapter 14 of Designing Artificial Compassion Technology, I suggest that each user have a customizable software agent that interfaces between them and the net, as well as apps, forms, etc. I created an architecture that is a spin off of the one used for interfacing between classified and unclassified documents at LLNL, although they do not use the idea of a software agent. (my background is in software agents, so it readily comes to mind)  

The agent's job is to be a guard rail for you, and relates to security and data privacy, as well as interacting with other agents, and can filter text, mail, search results, media and so on for emotional and mental factors, and preferences

I haven't worked out all the details, but each agent is part of an ecosystem, it could register with DOI, or an ODI, https://www.atera.com/glossary/object-identifier-oid/ and so on.

If it seems too risky to latch on to "agents" then just software guardrails might do. The concept applies to individuals, groups, institutions, etc.

Admittedly, this will create a silo, but we almost have that now, but without any real care about or protection for individual users.

emails.title: Preserving Open Letters with Bitcoin Ordinals
emails.author: Anon
emails.subject: Elevating the Need to Fix the Internet: A Strategic Opportunity with the Changing of the Guard
emails.body:
---- On Tue, 29 Oct 2024 20:09:01 -0700 Anon wrote ----

Hi Dave,

Thanks for your thoughtful response and the reading list. I appreciate the perspective you bring regarding Bitcoin’s socio-political implications. However, I wanted to clarify that my suggestion involves leveraging Bitcoin’s new information layer—something that may not be addressed in the sources you referenced.

As of January 2023, Casey Rodarmor introduced Ordinal Theory, which enables data—beyond financial transactions—to be stored as digital artifacts on the blockchain. This makes the data uncensorable, immutable, and accessible for the foreseeable future.

Just to be clear, I wasn’t proposing that Bitcoin be mentioned in the open letter itself. My suggestion to post the letter on Bitcoin’s blockchain is about preserving the message for posterity, not promoting Bitcoin ideologically as a currency. Given the cost of storage on Bitcoin, it's best suited for small amounts of data, such as identifiers and critical text fragments—which makes it ideal for something like an open letter.

We’d also post the letter on Medium (or a dedicated webpage), LinkedIn, and promote it across social media channels. Using Bitcoin Ordinals, however, as a permanent backup creates a permanent record that can’t be removed or altered. Additionally, with cryptocurrency and blockchain technologies becoming policy discussions, posting to Bitcoin could attract the attention of the next president’s cryptocurrency advisory council—an audience whose alignment could be strategically valuable.

I’d love to hear your thoughts on how we might ensure the letter reaches the right people, while also staying true to the values of a people-centered internet.

emails.author: Cindy Mason
emails.subject: Elevating the Need to Fix the Internet: A Strategic Opportunity with the Changing of the Guard

Cindy Mason

emails.title: Security and Privacy as Pillars of the Meta-Layer
emails.author: Anon
emails.subject: Elevating the Need to Fix the Internet: A Strategic Opportunity with the Changing of the Guard
emails.body:
---- On Wed, 30 Oct 2024 10:06:02 -0700 Anon wrote ----

Hi Vandana,

Thank you for sharing these thought-provoking insights. Your reflection on the design tradeoffs of the early internet really resonates—especially the idea that performance was prioritized over security. As you said, the assumptions made during those resource-constrained times no longer apply in today’s landscape, where the internet connects billions of people, devices, and objects. This is exactly why rethinking these assumptions and integrating security and privacy at the core of our systems—as you suggest—is incredibly timely and will be essential to building the next phase of the web.

You bring up some important themes around identity, privacy, and the tension between anonymity and authentication that are highly relevant to the meta layer concept. Below are some thoughts on how your insights align with what we’ve discussed—and where they might help shape our next steps.

1. Revisiting Internet Design Tradeoffs Through the Meta-Layer Lens

The internet’s foundational tradeoff between performance and security has had far-reaching consequences, particularly in IoT and personal data ecosystems. As you noted, early decisions assumed limited scope and localized networks, but today’s world has made those assumptions obsolete. The meta layer vision we’re working on offers a chance to build a meta-environment with security and privacy as integral elements, rather than afterthoughts.

The meta-layer's approach to privacy and decentralized control fits perfectly with your point: security and privacy can no longer be seen as optional costs, but as foundational pillars. This also ties into Cindy’s agent concept, which would give users the tools to manage data sovereignty and multiple identities dynamically, according to context.

2. Decentralized Identities: Balancing Authentication and Anonymity

I found your example about SIM authentication compelling—it highlights the delicate balance between authenticating users for security purposes without overexposing personal identities. This mirrors the multiple personas approach in Overweb, where users can choose between anonymous, pseudonymous, or authenticated interactions depending on the situation. There doesn’t need to be one identity for everything; different contexts can warrant different identities.

The meta-layer can integrate verifiable identities through decentralized identity (DID) protocols, allowing trust without compromising anonymity. This would let people and devices authenticate when necessary while still maintaining privacy where appropriate—exactly like your SIM example. With decentralized information ecosystems, the user remains in control of when, how, and to what extent their identities are used.

3. The Perceived Value of Security: A Barrier to Adoption

Your point about security being seen as a cost, not a competitive differentiator, is crucial. It’s something we’ve encountered in the conversation around decentralized platforms as well. Until companies and individuals see the value of security and privacy, adoption will be slow.

This is where decentralization can offer a new economic model—one where users, not centralized entities, own their data and control their interactions. With decentralized information architectures, users can monetize their data on their own terms, and companies that respect privacy can gain trust as a competitive advantage. There could also be incentives for adopting privacy-first principles rather than relying solely on regulations.

4. Incorporating Security into the Meta Layer and IoT

Your mention of IoT security reminds us that these issues aren’t limited to personal privacy—every connected device in our lives becomes a node in the larger ecosystem, and every weak link becomes a risk. Authentication of IoT devices will need to follow the same principles as user identity: decentralized, contextual, and privacy-preserving.

Cindy’s software agent concept could extend to IoT—acting as an intermediary between the user and their devices, controlling what data flows between them, and ensuring that devices only interact with trusted networks. This distributed security model ensures that no single point of failure compromises the entire system.

5. Building Security-First, Privacy-First Ecosystems

You are absolutely right that security and privacy provisions need to become inherent elements in the meta layer, just as much as performance or usability. It will take both visionary leadership and collaborative innovation—as we saw with DARPA’s Clean Slate initiative and CUSTARD program—to design a system that balances privacy, trust, and accessibility.

With the meta-layer vision, we aim to rewrite the assumptions of the past, creating information ecosystems that enable trust through transparency and decentralization. Your insights help reinforce that privacy, identity, and security must be non-negotiable elements in this design.

Next Steps: Incorporating Your Ideas in Our Open Letter and Discussions

I’d love to continue exploring how we can integrate these ideas into our open letter to the next president. Your points about identity, authentication, and privacy-conscious design are exactly the kinds of principles we want to advocate for—a decentralized web that protects individual sovereignty while supporting trusted interactions.

Would you be open to collaborating further on this section of the letter or offering feedback as we draft the final version? 

Thank you again for your valuable insights—your perspective as a startup founder working on these real-world challenges brings practical grounding to these visionary discussions. I look forward to continuing the conversation!
